content:
# Exodus Chapter 37 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 108-109; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 80-81 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_037_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_037_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_037_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_037_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Ark of the Covenant (vv. 1-9)

**1** And Bezalel made the ark of acacia wood; two cubits and a half its length, and a cubit and a half its width, and a cubit and a half its height.

**2** And he overlaid it with pure gold from inside and from outside, and he made for it a rim of gold all around.

**3** And he cast for it four rings of gold on its four feet, and two rings on its one side and two rings on its second side.

**4** And he made poles of acacia wood, and he overlaid them with gold.

**5** And he brought the poles into the rings on the sides of the ark, to carry the ark.

**6** And he made an atonement-cover of pure gold; two cubits and a half its length and a cubit and a half its width.

**7** And he made two cherubim of gold -- of hammered-work he made them -- from the two ends of the atonement-cover:

**8** one cherub from this end and one cherub from that end; from the atonement-cover he made the cherubim from its two ends.

**9** And the cherubim were spreading wings upward, covering with their wings over the atonement-cover, and their faces each to his brother; toward the atonement-cover were the faces of the cherubim.

---

## The Table of the Presence (vv. 10-16)

**10** And he made the table of acacia wood; two cubits its length and a cubit its width and a cubit and a half its height.

**11** And he overlaid it with pure gold, and he made for it a rim of gold all around.

**12** And he made for it a border of a handbreadth all around, and he made a rim of gold for its border all around.

**13** And he cast for it four rings of gold, and he put the rings on the four corners which are at its four legs.

**14** Opposite the border were the rings -- holders for the poles to carry the table.

**15** And he made the poles of acacia wood, and he overlaid them with gold, to carry the table.

**16** And he made the vessels which are on the table -- its dishes and its ladles and its bowls and the pitchers by which it is poured out -- pure gold.

---

## The Lampstand of Pure Gold (vv. 17-24)

**17** And he made the lampstand of pure gold; of hammered-work he made the lampstand -- its base and its shaft, its cups, its knobs, and its blossoms were from it.

**18** And six branches going out from its sides -- three branches of the lampstand from its one side and three branches of the lampstand from its second side.

**19** Three cups shaped like almonds on the one branch -- a knob and a blossom -- and three cups shaped like almonds on the other branch -- a knob and a blossom; so for the six branches going out from the lampstand.

**20** And on the lampstand -- four cups shaped like almonds, its knobs and its blossoms.

**21** And a knob under the two branches from it, and a knob under the two branches from it, and a knob under the two branches from it -- for the six branches going out from it.

**22** Their knobs and their branches were from it; all of it one hammered-work of pure gold.

**23** And he made its lamps -- seven -- and its tongs and its fire-pans of pure gold.

**24** Of a talent of pure gold he made it and all its vessels.

---

## The Altar of Incense (vv. 25-28)

**25** And he made the altar of incense of acacia wood; a cubit its length and a cubit its width -- square -- and two cubits its height; from it were its horns.

**26** And he overlaid it with pure gold -- its top and its walls all around and its horns; and he made for it a rim of gold all around.

**27** And two rings of gold he made for it beneath its rim, on its two sides -- on its two flanks -- as holders for poles to carry it with them.

**28** And he made the poles of acacia wood, and he overlaid them with gold.

---

## The Sacred Oil and Incense (v. 29)

**29** And he made the holy anointing oil and the incense of the spices, pure -- the work of a perfumer.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Observations

The Leningrad Codex (B19A) presents Exodus 37 across pages 108-109 in the standard three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation. The chapter continues directly from the tabernacle curtains, boards, veil, and screen of chapter 36, with no petuhah break before verse 1. Bezalel is named only in verse 1; all subsequent verses use the anonymous third-person "he made" (va-ya'as), subordinating the craftsman to the craft. The text closely mirrors the instruction chapters (25:10-40, 30:1-10, 22-38), shifting from the imperative "you shall make" to the narrative "and he made." A masorah parva note is visible beside miqshah ("hammered-work") in verse 17, consistent with Masoretic attention to this technical term. The chapter is notably shorter than its instruction parallel, omitting prescriptive commands and theological declarations (e.g., 25:15-16, 22; 30:6-10) and recording only the physical construction. The plene and defective spellings are consistent with scribal practice throughout the codex.

The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, pp. 80-81) presents perhaps the most dramatic MT-LXX divergence in the entire tabernacle narrative at this point. The manuscript pages are headed CAP. XXVII and CAP. XXVIII, confirming the well-known divergence. The furniture-execution narrative of MT Exodus 37 -- the ark, table, lampstand, and incense altar -- is largely absent from the Vaticanus text at this location. The LXX subsumed the furniture construction into the instruction chapters (LXX Exodus 25-26), so that the instruction text serves double duty as both command and execution. The Vaticanus pages at this point contain material corresponding to MT 38:1-20 (the bronze altar, laver, and courtyard) and portions of the priestly garments narrative. The three-column uncial scriptio continua is clearly legible. Whether the LXX reflects an earlier, shorter Hebrew Vorlage or represents editorial condensation of a text similar to the MT remains a central question in Exodus text criticism. SOPHIA follows the MT throughout, as it preserves the theologically significant command-fulfillment architecture in which instruction and obedient execution form a literary diptych.

### Theological Architecture of the Chapter

Exodus 37 is the fulfillment counterpart to the instructions of Exodus 25:10-40 (ark, table, lampstand) and Exodus 30:1-10, 22-38 (incense altar, anointing oil, incense). The chapter follows the same sequence as the command text, and the near-verbatim repetition constitutes the Priestly writer's demonstration of exact obedience. What YHWH spoke on Sinai, Bezalel enacts in the camp. The literary architecture is itself theological: obedience is shown by correspondence, faithfulness by repetition.

**The Named and Unnamed Craftsman (v. 1):** Bezalel is named only once in this chapter -- "And Bezalel made the ark" -- and thereafter the subject is an anonymous "he." This literary pattern attributes all the sacred furniture to the hand of the Spirit-filled master craftsman (31:3; 35:31), whose name means "in the shadow of God." The man who works in God's shadow constructs the seat of God's presence. By contrast, the tabernacle structure (chapter 36) uses the plural "they made" in many verses, suggesting collective labor. The distinction implies that while the structural fabric could be woven by many hands, the sacred furniture required the singular skill of the chief artisan.

**The Sequence from Most Holy to Holy (vv. 1-29):** The order of construction moves outward from the innermost sanctuary: the ark and atonement-cover (the Most Holy Place), the table and lampstand (the Holy Place), the incense altar (at the boundary of the Holy Place before the veil), and finally the sacred substances (oil and incense). This inside-out sequence mirrors the instruction order, proceeding from God's throne outward toward the people.

**The Atonement-Cover as Theological Convergence (vv. 6-9):** The kapporet, made of pure gold without the wood-and-gold composite of the ark itself, is the single point where three theological realities converge: the stored law (the tablets inside the ark below), atoning mercy (the kapporet covering the law above), and divine revelation (YHWH speaking from between the cherubim above the kapporet). The cherubim, hammered from the same piece of gold, face one another -- "each to his brother" -- framing the space of divine encounter. That mercy and the guardians of divine holiness are one substance means atonement is not foreign to divine majesty but intrinsic to it.

**The Lampstand as Golden Tree (vv. 17-24):** The menorah is the most elaborately described object in the chapter. Its botanical vocabulary -- cups shaped like almonds, knobs, blossoms -- presents a stylized tree of light, an echo of Eden's tree of life placed within the sanctuary. The insistence that it was hammered from a single talent of pure gold (approximately 75 pounds) emphasizes organic unity: "all of it one hammered-work" (v. 22). The almond (shaqed) puns on "watching" (shoqed) -- the same wordplay God uses with Jeremiah (1:11-12): "I am watching over my word."

**The Incense Altar at the Boundary (vv. 25-28):** The incense altar marks the furthest point of regular priestly approach toward the Most Holy Place. Its ascending smoke represents prayer rising before YHWH (Psalm 141:2). The horns, integral to the structure ("from it were its horns"), receive the atoning blood on Yom Kippur (30:10), linking the incense altar to the sacrificial system.

**From Objects to Substances (v. 29):** The chapter closes with substances rather than structures -- the anointing oil and the incense -- marking a shift from spatial theology (where God dwells) to sensory theology (what fills the space). Both are the "work of a perfumer" (ma'aseh roqeakh), a distinct craft from metalwork, requiring the same precision that governs the dimensions of the furniture.

### Key Consensus Decisions

1. **"Atonement-cover" for kapporet (vv. 6-9):** EMET renders "atonement-cover," LOGOS renders "mercy seat," PNEUMA renders "mercy seat." SOPHIA adopts "atonement-cover" (EMET) because it preserves the Hebrew root k-p-r ("to cover/atone") in the English rendering, maintaining the etymological link to Yom Kippur ("Day of Atonement"). "Mercy seat" (following Tyndale/KJV) is a venerable rendering but introduces "mercy" (a relational concept) where the Hebrew specifies "atonement" (a ritual-sacrificial concept). The LXX's hilasterion ("place of propitiation") supports the atoning function. This decision is consistent with preserving Hebrew theological vocabulary in English.

2. **"Knobs" for kaphtorim (vv. 17-22):** EMET renders "knobs," LOGOS renders "buds," PNEUMA renders "buds." SOPHIA adopts "knobs" (EMET) because the Hebrew kaphtor designates a rounded, bulbous protrusion -- etymologically related to a "capital" or "knob" rather than a botanical "bud." While the lampstand description carries botanical imagery, the kaphtor is the structural ornament at the junction point, distinct from the perakh ("blossom") which is explicitly floral. "Bud" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) merges the kaphtor and perakh into a single botanical register, whereas the Hebrew distinguishes between the rounded protrusion (kaphtor) and the opening flower (perakh).

3. **"Brought" for va-yave (v. 5):** EMET renders "brought," LOGOS renders "put," PNEUMA renders "slid." SOPHIA adopts "brought" (EMET) because the Hebrew is the Hiphil of bo' ("to cause to come/bring"), describing the insertion of poles into rings. "Put" (LOGOS) is acceptable but less precise. "Slid" (PNEUMA) adds a manner of movement not specified in the Hebrew. The Hiphil of bo' indicates causative entry -- the poles were brought into the rings.

4. **"Border" for misgeret (vv. 12, 14):** EMET renders "border," LOGOS renders "frame," PNEUMA omits the technical term. SOPHIA adopts "border" (EMET) because misgeret (from sagar, "to close/enclose") designates a raised edge or rim enclosing the table surface, not a structural frame. The LXX's stephanen ("wreath/crown") in the instruction parallel supports an encircling border rather than a structural frame.

5. **"Pitchers" for qesavot (v. 16):** EMET renders "jugs," LOGOS renders "pitchers," PNEUMA renders "pitchers." SOPHIA adopts "pitchers" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority) because the Hebrew qesavot designates vessels for pouring libations, and "pitcher" more precisely conveys a vessel designed for pouring than the generic "jug."

6. **"Shaped like almonds" for meshuqqadim (vv. 19-20):** EMET renders "shaped-like-almonds," LOGOS renders "shaped like almond blossoms," PNEUMA renders "shaped like almond blossoms." SOPHIA adopts "shaped like almonds" (EMET, minus hyphens) because the Pual participle meshuqqadim means "almond-shaped" -- shaped like the almond fruit or flower. LOGOS and PNEUMA add "blossoms" (not explicit in the participle itself), narrowing the reference to flowers alone. The Hebrew leaves the precise referent (fruit, flower, or both) open.

7. **"Its base and its shaft" for yerekah ve-qanah (v. 17):** All three translators render yerekah as "base" and qanah as "shaft." SOPHIA concurs. The Hebrew yerekah ("its thigh/base") designates the heavy foot of the lampstand, and qanah ("its reed/shaft") designates the central stem from which the branches extend.

8. **"Fire-pans" for makhtoteiha (v. 23):** EMET renders "fire-pans," LOGOS renders "fire pans," PNEUMA renders "trays." SOPHIA adopts "fire-pans" (EMET/LOGOS) because the Hebrew makhtot (from khatah, "to snatch fire/coals") specifically designates vessels for handling live coals and ash, not generic trays. The compound "fire-pans" preserves the etymological connection to fire.

9. **"Tongs" for malqakheiha (v. 23):** EMET renders "tongs," LOGOS renders "tongs," PNEUMA renders "wick trimmers." SOPHIA adopts "tongs" (EMET/LOGOS) because the Hebrew malqakhayim (dual of leqakh, from laqakh, "to take/grasp") designates a grasping instrument. "Wick trimmers" (PNEUMA) specifies the function but the Hebrew names only the form -- an instrument for taking/grasping.

10. **"The holy anointing oil" for shemen ha-mishkhah qodesh (v. 29):** EMET renders "the oil of the holy anointing," LOGOS renders "the holy anointing oil," PNEUMA renders "the sacred anointing oil." SOPHIA adopts "the holy anointing oil" (LOGOS) because the construct chain places qodesh ("holy") as a qualifier of the entire phrase, and "holy anointing oil" is the most natural English word order. EMET's "oil of the holy anointing" follows the Hebrew word order but produces awkward English. PNEUMA's "sacred" is a synonym for "holy" but qodesh is consistently rendered "holy" throughout the SOPHIA translation.

11. **"The incense of the spices" for qetoret ha-sammim (v. 29):** EMET renders "the incense of the spices," LOGOS renders "the pure fragrant incense," PNEUMA renders "the pure, fragrant incense." SOPHIA adopts "the incense of the spices" (EMET) because it preserves the Hebrew construct chain qetoret ha-sammim. LOGOS and PNEUMA replace "of the spices" with "fragrant," which is an interpretive substitution -- sammim means "spices/aromatics," not "fragrant" (an adjective). The word tahor ("pure") modifies qetoret and is rendered separately.

12. **"Work of a perfumer" for ma'aseh roqeakh (v. 29):** EMET renders "work of a perfumer," LOGOS renders "work of a perfumer," PNEUMA renders "work of a master perfumer." SOPHIA adopts "work of a perfumer" (EMET/LOGOS) because the Hebrew roqeakh means "perfumer/apothecary" without an added intensifier. PNEUMA's "master" is not present in the Hebrew.

13. **"On its two flanks" for al shnei tsiddav (v. 27):** EMET renders "on its two flanks," LOGOS renders "on its two flanks," PNEUMA paraphrases as "on two of its opposite sides." SOPHIA adopts "on its two flanks" (EMET/LOGOS) because the Hebrew tsiddav means "its sides/flanks" and the construct is a simple possessive. PNEUMA's "opposite" adds a spatial relationship not explicit in the Hebrew, though it is inferred from the two-pole arrangement.

14. **Vessel order in v. 16:** EMET lists the vessels as "dishes, ladles, bowls, pitchers," while LOGOS lists "plates, ladles, pitchers, bowls." The Hebrew order is qe'arot (dishes/plates), kappot (ladles), menaqiyyot (bowls), qesavot (pitchers). SOPHIA follows the Hebrew order: dishes, ladles, bowls, pitchers. LOGOS transposes bowls and pitchers; SOPHIA corrects to MT order.
